frameworks.name: Bayesian confidence
frameworks.one_line: Confidence equals posterior probability that the decision is correct.

frameworks.name: qualitative signatures approach
frameworks.one_line: Pattern-based diagnostics proposed to test whether an observer is Bayesian.

core_question: Are the proposed qualitative signatures of Bayesian confidence valid necessary or sufficient tests of the Bayesian model?
key_assumptions: Bayesian observer assigns confidence as posterior probability of correctness
Signatures derived under specific stimulus-distribution and noise regimes
Class-conditioned stimulus distributions may or may not overlap
manipulations: Overlap vs. non-overlap of class-conditioned stimulus distributions
Low vs. high levels of internal noise
Stimulus magnitude on correct vs. incorrect trials
key_findings: 0.75 average confidence at neutral evidence only holds with non-overlapping distributions and very low noise
Divergence of correct/incorrect confidence with stimulus magnitude only holds with non-overlap or high noise
Both signatures contain hidden assumptions limiting their applicability
Neither signature is necessary nor sufficient for Bayesian confidence
interpretations: Qualitative signatures are unreliable shortcuts for diagnosing Bayesian confidence
Quantitative model comparison is required to identify computations underlying confidence
